findings.description: R-1 mandates a microservices architecture with at least 6 services and Kubernetes without stating the current scale, team size, or operational problem that demands this complexity. The number '6' is arbitrary and prescriptive rather than derived from any stated need.
findings.evidence: The background says only 'growing and needs a modern platform to handle orders at scale' — no current order volume, no SLA, no team size. Removing the 6-service / Kubernetes mandate leaves the functional goal intact; nothing functional breaks.

findings.description: R-2 requires Apache Kafka with a 3-broker cluster for all inter-service communication before any throughput, latency, or decoupling need is identified. This is a solution looking for a problem.
findings.evidence: No event volume, no ordering guarantees, no async processing requirement is stated anywhere. If every team applied this pattern every time, every small project would carry a Kafka cluster. Removing Kafka does not remove any stated business requirement.

findings.description: R-3 and R-7 are in direct conflict: R-3 mandates a single shared PostgreSQL database with per-service schemas, while R-7 mandates that each service owns its data and must not share database tables. A single database with shared schemas violates service autonomy; true service autonomy requires separate databases.
findings.location: R-3: Data Storage vs R-7: Service Autonomy
findings.evidence: R-3: 'single PostgreSQL 15 database with logical partitioning per service schema'. R-7: 'Each service must own its data and shall not share database tables with other services.' A shared physical database makes schema-level isolation fragile and contradicts the autonomy principle. Left unchanged this guarantees implementation disputes and runtime coupling.

findings.description: R-8 mandates Redis 7.x with a minimum of 16 GB allocated memory for 'all caching needs' with no identified cache use case, hit-rate target, or data size estimate to justify the allocation.
findings.evidence: No session size, no hot-data volume, no latency SLA is stated. Removing the 16 GB floor does not break any stated business requirement. Applied universally this pattern would pre-allocate large memory budgets on every project regardless of load.

findings.description: R-5 specifies an exact React/Next.js/TypeScript stack without referencing any UI requirement (SSR latency, SEO, accessibility) that would justify the choice over alternatives.
findings.evidence: Removing the tech-stack prescription from R-5 leaves no stated functional frontend requirement unmet. The requirement says nothing about page-load targets or SEO needs that would distinguish Next.js SSR from other approaches.
